title: 5 2 Homogeneous second order ODEs
body:
5.2 HOMOGENEOUS SECOND ORDER ODES


The homogeneous second order differential equation with constant coefficients

                                     ay 00 + by 0 + cy = 0

                    is solved by first forming the auxiliary equation

                                     aλ2 + bλ + c = 0.

    The auxiliary equation is not an ODE. It is a quadratic equation with two roots
                  λ1 and λ2 that determine the solution to the ODE:

                   • λ1 , λ2 ∈ R, λ1 6= λ2 =⇒ y = Aeλ1 t + Beλ2 t

                   • λ1 , λ2 ∈ R, λ1 = λ2 =⇒ y = (At + B)eλ1 t

            • λ1 , λ2 ∈ C, λ1 , λ2 = r ± is =⇒ y = ert (A cos(st) + B sin(st))



Second order ODEs are in general more difficult to solve than first order ODEs. In this
course we will deal with the special case of linear second order ODEs with constant
coefficients. That is, they take the form

                                   ay 00 + by 0 + cy = r(t)

where a, b, c are (real) constants and r(t) is a function of t. In this lecture we will deal
with the homogeneous case with r(t) = 0. In the next lecture we will look at the case
when there is a function on the right-hand side.

Keep in mind that all second order ODEs must have two independent arbitrary constants
in solution and hence need two initial conditions to solve for the constants. The method
of solution for second order problems is totally different from first order techniques. But
the good news is that we don’t need to do any integration!

We begin with an important property of linear ODEs. We will prove this result for second
order ODEs but it is true for any order.

Superposition of solutions: Let y1 (t) and y2 (t) be two independent solutions of the
linear second order differential equation

                                    ay 00 + by 0 + cy = 0.

The the general solution to the ODE is

                                  y(x) = Ay1 (t) + By2 (t)

for arbitrary constants A, B.

                                              1
 Proof: First we note that if y1 (t) and y2 (t) are solutions then

                     ay100 + by10 + cy1 = 0 and ay200 + by20 + cy2 = 0.




                                                                                       F

Now we make an assumption about what the solution might look like. We assume that

                                          y = eλt .

We will not give a value for λ yet: the value will emerge from the analysis. Notice that
each derivative of y now just brings down a factor of λ:

                                 y 0 = λeλt       y 00 = λ2 eλt

Now substitute these into the linear homogeneous second order ODE

                                aλ2 eλt + bλeλt + ceλt = 0.

We can safely cancel out the common factor of eλt because it can never be zero. This
leaves us with
                                 aλ2 + bλ + c = 0.
Notice that the derivatives have disappeared. We are no longer dealing with a differential
equation anymore! This is a quadratic equation for the unknown parameter λ. It is called
the auxiliary equation (also called the characteristic equation). Note that we do not need
to derive this equation each time — we can just write it down, replacing each derivative
with a power of λ.

The solution to the auxiliary equation will be the roots of the quadratic equation. Recall
that in general there are three cases for the roots of a quadratic equation:

   • Two real roots, not equal to each other: λ1 , λ2 ∈ R with λ1 6= λ2 .

   • Two real roots, equal to each other: λ1 , λ2 ∈ R with λ1 = λ2 .

   • Two complex roots, a conjugate pair: λ1 , λ2 ∈ C with λ1 = r + is, λ2 = r − is.

Each case will give a different kind of solution to the differential equation. We will only
prove the first case in detail.




                                              2
 Proof:




                                                                             F
Example 1 Solve each of the following second order D.E.s:
a) y 00 − 5y 0 + 6y = 0

b) y 00 − 6y 0 + 9y = 0

  d2 y  dy
c) 2 − 6 + 58y = 0
  dx    dx




    F a) y = Ae2t + Be3t b) y = (At + B)e3t c) y = e3x (A cos(7x) + B sin(7x)) F

                                        3
                                         R = -ky
                                                            V = y’

                                                                   y
                0                                Fric = -cy’


                            FREE OSCILLATIONS
To understand the different kinds of solutions we can get from second order linear homo-
geneous ODEs, it is useful to look at an analogous physical system. Consider the problem
of a mass m > 0 attached to a spring so that it can move back and forth in the y-direction.
The velocity of the mass is y 0 = dy/dt and the acceleration is y 00 = d2 y/dt2 . The forces
acting on the mass are:

   • A restoring force R = −ky, where k > 0 is the spring constant. This force tries to
     restore the mass to its equilibrium position at y = 0.

   • A frictional force f = −cy 0 , where c > 0 is the damping constant. This force tries
     to slow down the mass so that it has zero velocity.

From Newton’s law we have F = my 00 , where F = R + f is the sum of the forces on the
mass. Rearranging the equation gives

                                   my 00 + cy 0 + ky = 0.

This is a second order linear homogeneous ODE. The solutions of this equation describe
the movement of the mass as it oscillates. We say that these are free oscillations because
there is no right-hand side — this is a homogeneous equation. In the next lecture we will
look at the inhomogeneous case, which gives rise to forced oscillations.

Seeking solutions y = Aeλt gives the auxiliary equation

                                    mλ2 + cλ + k = 0,

where m, c and k are all positive numbers. The roots of this quadratic equation are
                  1 h     √          i                     1 h      √         i
          λ1 =              2
                      −c + c − 4mk         and      λ2 =               2
                                                               −c − c − 4mk .
                2m                                        2m
                √             √
Observe that c2 − 4mk ≤ c2 = c implying that when λ1 and λ2 are real they will
always be less than or equal to zero. Four cases arise, depending on the magnitude of the
frictional coefficient c:

  1. c2 > 4mk. This is called overdamping
                               √             since the damping or frictional coefficient
     c is large compared with 2 mk. In this case λ1 and λ2 are both real and negative.
     The solution is
                                   y = Aeλ1 t + Beλ2 t
     which decays to zero as t −→ ∞.

                                             4
 Figure 1: Solutions to y 00 + cy 0 + y = 0 for overdamping (c = 3), critical damping (c = 2),
underdamping (c = 1) and no damping (c = 0).



  2. c2 = 4mk. This is called critical damping and λ1 = λ2 so the solution is

                                      y = (A + Bt)e−ct/2m .

     The solution also decays to 0 as t −→ ∞. In fact, it will decay to zero faster than
     the case of underdamping, even though the frictional coefficient c is smaller in this
     case.
                                                                     √
  3. c2 < 4mk. This is called underdamping as c is smaller than 2 mk. Then

                                λ1 = −r + is,       λ2 = −r − is,

      where r = c/2m and s = (k/m − c2 /4m2 )1/2 . (This is left as an exercise for the
      reader.) The solutions are of the form

                                y = e−rt (A cos(st) + B sin(st)) .

      This represents decaying oscillations. The cosine and sine functions oscillate between
      1 and −1. But they are multiplied by the decaying exponential e−rt . Thus, the
      oscillations are eventually killed off by friction.

  4. c = 0. This is the idealised case of no damping. The roots of the auxiliary
     equation are simply
                                λ1 = +is,    λ2 = −is,
               p
     where s = k/m. The solutions are

                                   y = A cos (st) + B sin (st) .

      This represents an undamped system that oscillates back and forth forever.




                                             5
      Example 2 Consider the differential equation

                                             y 00 + cy 0 + y = 0.

     What value of the damping constant c produces a) Overdamping b) Critical Damping c)
     Underdamping d) No damping . Find and identify the solutions for c = 1 and c = 3.




F Overdamping: c > 2, Critical damping: c = 2, Underdamping: 0 < c < 2, No damping: c = 0. F
                                                               1
                                                                         √             √
              F c=1        =⇒     Underdamping and y = e− 2 t (A cos( 23 t) + B sin( 23 t)) F
                                                                  h √ i              h √ i
                                                              − 21 3+ (5) t      − 12 3− (5) t
             F c=3        =⇒      Overdamping and y = A e                     +B e               F
